content.full_text:
Re
Farn Polrrrcer PnacrrcEs ConaurssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
January 13,2005
Paul F. Walker, Councilmember
City of LaPalma
7812Kelly Circle
LaPalma, CA 90623
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-04-254
DearMr. Walker:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').r The Commission will rrot udrir"
with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bXSXA).) Therefore, nothing in this
letter should be constnred to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and
any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this
letter provides advice regarding the specific facts of your unique situation and should not
be construed to apply generally.
QUESTION
May you receive reimbursement for a mailing and newspaper advertisement
supporting three candidates which was paid by your own personal funds?
CONCLUSION
Yes.
FACTS
You are an outgoing member of the La Palma City Council and wanted to thank
your constituents for allowing you to serve them for eight years, and also voice your
support for three new candidates. To this end, you placed an advertisement in your local
I Government Code sections 81000 - 9rc14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
6EG


File No. A-04-254
Page No. 2
paper and sent a mailing for which you paid atotal of $899 from your own personal
account. You state in your letter that your committee is closed.
You work for Orange County Fire Authority as afte apparatus engineer. One of
your co-workers, who is in charge of a local political action committee ("PAC'),
overheard your story and stated that they could possibly reimburse you for the
advertisement and mailing with PAC money.
ANALYSIS
Under the Act, apayment2 received by a candidate3 is generally classified as a
"contribution" or a"gifr." A "contribution" is a payment made for political purposes
rather than for personal purposes. (Section 82015.) Section 820I5(b)(2) states that a
payrnent is made for "political purposes" if it is received by or made at the behest of a
candidate unless the criteria in either subparagraph (A) or (B) below are satisfied:
"(A) Full and adequate consideration is received from the candidate.
"(B) It is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the payment was
made for purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy for elective office.
The following types of payrnents are presumed to be for purposes
unrelated to a candidate's candidacy for elective office:
(i) A payment made principally forpersonal purposes, in which case it
may be considered a gift under the provisions of Secti on 82028 . . .."
In addition, the definition of "conkibution" excludes payments received by a
candidate for personal purposes. (Regulation 1 32 I 5(c)(l a).)
6(Gift" is defined, in pertinent part, as "any paynent that confers a personal
benefit on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not
received . . . ." (Section 82028(a).)
You paid for the advertisement and mailings from personal funds and not from the
funds in your controlled committee.o Thus, the proposed payment by the PAC, although
2 "'Palrment' means a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other
rendering of money, property, services or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible." (Section
82044.)
' The Commission considers all elected officeholders as "candidates," even during a non-election
year. (See section 82007 and In re Lui (1987) l0 FPPC Ops. 10.)
o Your letter states that your committee is closed; however, it appears from the Secretary of
State's website that it is, in fact, still open. You have not asked whether or not you were permitted to make
the initial expenditure for the mailing from your personal funds and we could not answer a question based
onpast conduct (Regulation 18329(bX8XA). Sections of the Actpertinent to that issue, howevet are
sections 8951 1.5(a)(1) and (2) as well as regulation 18525.


File No. A-04-254
Page No. 3
ostensibly having the political pu{pose of reimbursing you for personal costs incurred in
the production and dissernination of the communications, is not a contribution to you.
From our telephone conversation, you indicated that there was no prior agreeme,lrt or
understanding between you and the PAC that would have led you to believe you would
be reimbursed. Therefore, it appears that the primary pu{pose of the proposed payment
by the PAC is to relieve you of the financial burden of paying for the communication.
Next, we turn our attention to your actual question: whether or not you can acfually
receive the payment. As a publig official you may receive up to $360 in gifts from a
single source in a calend ar yeax,s and, if you have Ieft office and are no longer a public
official, the gift limit will no longerbe applicable. Whether ornot the PAC is able to use
its campaign funds to make a gift to you is a separate question the PAC must consider. If
an authorized representative of the PAC wishes to ask for advice, that representative may
fax the request to (916) 327-2026 and we will be happy to provide assistance.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660' 
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
t---fl* 
cAata €
By: Adrianne Korchmaros
&f CAU-<n/? D
Political Reform Consultant
AKJg
I:\Adviceltrs\04-254
s The Act provides that "[nJo elected state ofEcer, elected officer of a local government agenCy,
or ottrer individual specified in Section 87200 shall accept gifts from any single source in any calendar year
with a total value of more than [$360]." (Section 89503(a); also see section 895 03 (f); regulation I 89 40.2.)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May you receive reimbursement for a mailing and newspaper advertisement
supporting three candidates which was paid by your own personal funds?

CONCLUSION: Yes.
